data.title: US Steel: Nippon Has Reason To Drop Its Bid After Trump Whipsaw
data.description:
US Steel (NYSE:X) has faced immense volatility this year due to the combined whipsaw of global financial markets and its on-again/off-again potential deal with Nippon Steel (OTCPK:NPSCY). From January through March, many investors shifted their view, speculating that Nippon's bid may be successful.

In February, Nippon reiterated its intention to purchase all of US Steel for $14B, engaging in negotiations with the US. Days before that, President Trump mentioned that Nippon may invest in it without buying it outright. In March, US Steel saw some upside after Trump stated plans to double the tax on Canadian steel and aluminum imports, retaliating against Ontario's electricity tariffs in the US. Around 20% of steel imports into the US come from Canada, though this plan was halted shortly after proposed.

Tariffs have become a significant factor for US Steel. Trump claims that the company does not require foreign investment now that tariffs defend the industry. As things currently stand, all steel imports are subject to a 25% tariff. China, which imports a minimal amount into the US, is subject to a 125% tariff. Theoretically, this should benefit US Steel as a domestic producer, as around a quarter of the steel used in the US comes from foreign sources.

Early this month, US Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick met with Nippon and activist investor Ancora to discuss the deal. If the Nippon deal collapses, Ancora will offer a $6B to $7B investment. On Monday, US Steel surged 13% after Trump ordered a national security review of the Nippon deal. The Committee of Foreign Investment in the US is looking to determine whether or not the deal would be a strategic issue after President Biden blocked it earlier this year.

These gains reversed Thursday morning after Trump said, "We love Japan, but US Steel is a very special company," causing X to open 12% lower. As a result, Ancora dropped its offer. I speculate that Ancora may stay away from the stock in light of ongoing general market volatility and rising recession risk.

US Steel is trading around the midpoint of its pre-deal and bid price range. In early January, I offered the bearish view that "U.S. Steel's Long-Term Survival Is Uncertain Without Capital Infusion." This was before Trump pivoted toward potential support for the deal and pursued substantial steel protection tariffs. Since then, X has risen by about 30%, primarily due to both factors. Short interest on US Steel is slightly high at 7.3%; however, I noted that shorting the stock in January was likely unwise due to the likelihood of regulatory factors whipsawing it.

Given this volatility, it seems like an ideal time to provide an updated analysis and outlook for the company. Key factors include its valuation, the odds of a deal, the impact of tariffs, and the broader macroeconomic trend.

US Steel No Longer Worst in Industry

Before the US Steel-Nippon merger talk, X traded around $30 to $35 per share. If the deal goes through, X is set to rise to the bid price of $55 per share. It traded close to that price in early 2024, as the deal seemed likely until Biden took a negative view on the merger. Trump has shared this opinion, viewing a US Steel sale to Japan as an issue of national security.

US Steel is a smaller producer today than in the past. Nucor (NUE) is the largest with 21M tonnes, Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF) is second at ~17M tonnes, and US Steel is third at ~16M tonnes. US Steel has much less efficient factories than Nucor, with excessive reliance on blast furnaces. Due to lackluster demand growth and lower global steel prices, US Steel has struggled financially. In my view, its long-term survival is limited, given that it is generally unlikely that steel consumption surges in the US while its profitability is low compared to Nucor.

That said, in early April, I published "Cleveland-Cliffs: Tariffs Likely Harming Domestic Steel Demand." Although US Steel is not in a great position, Cleveland-Cliffs is worse due to not only its inefficient operations but also its excessive leverage. This analysis shifted my outlook for US Steel; if one major American steel company may go under, I believe Cleveland-Cliffs is now at materially greater risk than US Steel.

US Steel's profit margins are compressing with the rest of the industry. Its liabilities are around 50% of its total enterprise value, while its debt-to-EBITDA is elevated at 3X. That said, Cleveland-Cliff's is in a worse position with negative margins, extreme debt-to-EBITDA (due to a low EBITDA), and excessive liabilities-to-EV. See below:

Luckily, Trump's precedent from his past administration provides insight into how steel imports should change with the return of 25% tariffs. After Biden ended this policy, steel imports surged from around $9B a quarter to a peak of $16B before reversing back to $12B. The primary cause for lower imports is a cyclical economic decline in steel demand, driven by lower construction spending growth, a contracting manufacturing PMI, and slower vehicle production. As a result, US Capacity Utilization of steel has waned from a peak of nearly 80% to almost 70%:

I expect tariffs to drive steel imports back down to pre-2021 levels. Not all steel imports should end, but a significant reduction may exist. On the one hand, that benefits domestic producers like US Steel.

However, as I noted in the CLF article, tariffs in general have a negative impact on the global manufacturing supply chain, and domestic steel consumption may fall as much as or more than steel imports. The steel demand trend was already slightly negative over recent years. The HRC steel price jumped around 35% due to tariffs to $920. Although that should aid profitability, demand may turn lower due to higher prices and the broader supply chain issue created by tariffs.

The US does not import many vehicles from China, but a huge number of components used in US vehicles do come from China. This is likely also true for construction and most domestic manufacturing. Even if Trump aims to support domestic manufacturing through tariffs, I expect manufacturing activity will decline this year because so many manufacturing input parts come from China or other countries that may be exposed to higher tariffs. Of course, retaliation against US tariffs will limit exports, such as cars, the United States' sixth most significant export.

My view is speculative, but if we consider the supply chain impact of tariffs, the complete lack of capital investment expectations (factory development, etc), the expected 2.4% decline in the US real GDP, and the potential recession, it seems that domestic steel demand will decline sharply this year. I believe that this will likely offset the positive impact of tariffs.

For this reason, I have a generally bearish outlook on the steel industry. That said, Cleveland-Cliffs is the one with the most significant risk exposure. In the long run, I still believe X will require some considerable investment to improve the efficiency of its factories to make it competitive with Nucor. However, assuming a recession will drive steel demand lower and force closures of outdated steel mills, Cleveland-Cliffs will very likely face the most significant loss. I think Cleveland-Cliffs will need to shutter operations until the supply-demand imbalance is closed. Indeed, Cleveland has already begun to pursue layoffs, while US Steel has warned it will if the Nippon deal fails, though that was before the tariff impact.

Trump Wants an Investment, Not Acquisition

The bulk of X's volatility stems from the on-and-off deal speculation coming from Nippon and Trump. Crucially, if the global economy is entering a hard recession where the law of "cash is king" returns, I do not expect Nippon will necessarily remain so keen on completing the acquisition. For now, Nippon is very focused on the deal, but given Ancora backed out, I would not be surprised to see Nippon be less keen on completing the agreement if its managers become concerned about a global recession.

Trump's position is unclear, but he has historically supported the idea of Nippon investing in US Steel without a controlling stake. I believe it's fair to say that there is a sense of national pride regarding the US Steel issue because it is a legacy name-brand American company, which Trump does not want to go to Japan. Although US Steel's long-term potential is limited without a significant investment, its immediate risk does not appear high in markets if we compare its position to Cleveland-Cliffs. In other words, Trump does not necessarily need to approve the deal to save US jobs.

Nippon's Bid is Higher Than Reasonable

When Nippon placed a bid on US Steel, the steel industry was performing much better with high prices and resurgent demand as COVID lockdown policies ended. The steel industry was in a fundamentally better position with more supportive valuations.

However, over the past year, we've seen a sharp decline in Nucor's valuation, with its two-year forward "EV/EBTIDA" falling to 5.1X. NUE and X are similarly valued from a "P/E" standpoint, but X has a higher two-year forward "EV/EBITDA" at about 6.2X, given a rise in deal odds over recent months. Based on its historical EPS, CLF would have a low valuation, but is expected to lose money this year. Accounting for its debt leverage using forward "EV/EBITDA", its valuation is similar to Nucor's. See below:

The two-year ahead "EV/EBIDA" valuation is particularly useful because it accounts for the impact of debt leverage and expected changes in earnings. Of course, I believe that all steel companies will have lower EBITDA two years from now than the analysis consensus assumes. Still, even at consensus outlooks, US Steel's valuation is, to me, excessive. For its forward "EV/EBITDA to be ~5.2X (close to its peers), its enterprise value must be ~16% lower or ~$11B. After accounting for the net debt of about $3B, it shifts its market capitalization target to ~$8B, about 22% lower or a $32 share price.

The Bottom Line

I am bearish on US Steel because I doubt Trump will allow the Nippon deal to go through. Further, I think it may be possible that Nippon will become less aggressive in trying to complete this deal as it grapples with a recessionary global manufacturing dynamic. Additionally, although US Steel may see initial support from tariffs, I do not think it will benefit the firm long-term if tariffs accelerate lower US manufacturing activity due to a general recession and higher input costs on Chinese-made components.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: NMI Holdings: Hold On Until Market Conditions Improve
data.description:
At the end of 2024, there were warning signs that the US Housing Market was heading into choppy waters. At the start of 2025, the turbulence has officially arrived. When compared to household income, homes have never been more expensive in the US than they are today. Delinquency rates are rising, and interest rates are stuck in the sixes with a great deal of uncertainty because of a possible recession in the US.

Amid these macro conditions, NMI Holdings (NASDAQ:NMIH), a provider of private mortgage insurance, or PMI, appears to be standing firm. Of all of the publicly traded PMI providers, NMIH appears to be in a strong financial position to increase in market value when the housing market is on better footing. I am rating the stock as a hold, but I certainly think this is a company worth watching when the market headwinds taper off.

Company Overview

With a current market cap of just over $2.5 billion, NMI Holdings is the smallest of the five pure-play PMI providers. The other four are Enact Holdings (ACT), Essent Group (ESNT), MGIC Investment Corporation (MTG), and Radian Group (RDN). With headquarters in Emeryville, CA, NMI Holdings was founded in 2012. This date is noteworthy because it means that the portfolio of mortgages that it insures does not have the lingering remnants of the housing crises that started just a few years before its founding.

Private mortgage insurance guarantees payments to lenders when home buyers are unable to put down at least 20% of the purchase price of a home. If a homeowner defaults on payments, PMI ensures that lenders can be made whole. As I discussed in an earlier article about Radian Group, PMI is a commoditized service. There is little difference in the terms of the policies from any of the major providers, and these policies are chosen by the lenders, not the borrowers. Therefore, barring a complete collapse of one of the other providers, it is challenging for any of the companies like NMIH or RDN to make large gains in market share over a short period of time.

NMIH has the smallest policy portfolio in the industry with $210.2 billion of insurance-in-force as of the last reported quarter. However, its portfolio also has the highest quality of customers. The average FICO score of an NMIH policyholder is 757, 10 points better than its nearest competitor. Better credit scores from customers should lead to lower risk for defaults.

Housing Market Issues

The biggest hurdle that the housing market has to face is a pending recession, something that would likely impact most every other sector of our economy. The Atlanta Fed is predicting that there will be a real GDP decline in the first quarter of -2.8%. A slower economy means great chances for mortgage delinquencies and defaults.

As this probable recession nears, the likelihood of mortgage defaults increases. This date falls on the backdrop of home prices that have never been higher, both on a seasonally adjusted basis and when compared to household income. Consider the two graphs below.

On the one hand, the less affordable homes become, the more likely people are to need private mortgage insurance. That fact is reinforced by the fact that cash purchases actually declined last year according to Redfin. Cash purchases are more likely to be made by investors, and investors were less likely to buy homes last year because of rising rates.

There are really only two ways that this affordability gap for housing can be fixed in the short term. Home prices have to fall significantly, which would be problematic for PMI providers if defaults were rising. Or, household income has to rise significantly, which would be inflationary and likely cause interest rates to rise. This would also be a negative for the housing market.

The current picture of delinquencies is not troubling yet, but there are signs of possible trouble ahead, especially with FHA and VA loans. The Mortgage Bankers Association recently noted that while overall delinquencies are rising at a relatively slow pace, “FHA and VA delinquencies are increasing at a faster pace.” By the end of 2024, “the spread between the FHA and conventional delinquency rates reached 841 basis points, while the VA and conventional spread was 208 basis points.”

What Makes NMI Holdings Top Quality

NMIH stock has fallen just over 10% year-to-date. This pullback has given the stock a much more reasonable valuation both on a historical basis and on the basis of a comparison with its peers. Several of the company’s financial metrics place it at or near the top when compared to the other PMI providers. Consider the following statistics:

  • The company’s return on assets after the last quarter was 11.45% and its return on equity was 17.38%. The ROA number is second among the five PMI providers, trailing MGIC Investment Corporation by only 21 basis points. Its ROE number is 248 basis points ahead of its closest peer.
  • NMIH currently trades at a PE ratio of 7.4, below its five-year average and below the average for the other players in this category.
  • Sales growth for the company over the last year was 12.43%, the highest among its peers.
  • Earnings growth for the year ahead is expected to be tepid, at best, at 2.5%, but that number represents the only positive number from all five PMI providers. If earnings grow as expected, and the price stays flat, this is a stock that would have a PE of 6.45 in 2026.
  • Last fall, the stock traded at a price-to-book value of 1.61. As of the last quarterly report, that number has dropped to 1.16. This has been caused by a decrease in the price of the stock coinciding with an increase in book value.
  • Investment income for the company continues to rise at an impressive rate. Investment income for Q4 2024 was $22.7 million, almost 25% more than the same quarter of the previous year. This is especially impressive when the company's investment portfolio is made up primarily of corporate debt as shown in NMIH's last quarterly report.

One small negative related to NMIH is that it does not currently pay a dividend. All other PMI providers do with yields ranging from 2.3 to 3.33%. I do not see this as a major detraction from owning NMIH, but the dividends give the other stocks a little added cushion in the market when there is no price appreciation.

However, the company is returning capital to shareholders through stock repurchases. NMIH announced in its last quarterly report that its board has authorized “an additional $250 million share repurchase plan effective through December 31, 2027.” At the current market cap of the company, that represents about 10% of all its outstanding shares.

Assessment of Investment Risks

There is certainly a chance that interest rates will fall faster than expected this year and home-buying activity will, in turn, take a welcome uptick. However, there is also a chance that the recession prediction discussed earlier in this article will come true, driving down home prices and creating a new, albeit hopefully smaller, version of the financial crisis seen in the US 16 years ago.

I also have some concern over the company’s exposure to the real estate market in California and Florida, albeit for different reasons. California is the company's top state for policies, topping out at just over 10% of NMI Holdings' policy portfolio as of December 31, 2024. California currently leads all states with a rate of 2.38% of homes in delinquencies or foreclosures.

Florida is third on its list of policy locations, and the market there appears to be taking a rather sharp turn for the worse this year. Higher home insurance rates are an added cost for buyers in that market. Over the last 12 months, the number of homes in Florida that are not current, either because of delinquencies or foreclosures, has increased 16.72%, giving that state the highest rate of increase in the country.

The Bottom-Line

Having reviewed all of the five publicly traded PMI providers discussed in this report, I believe that NMI Holdings represents the “best in class” of the group. That being said, I think the next six to 12 months for the housing market will have more bad news than good. We are still a long way from an interest rate level that will bring large numbers of buyers back into the market, and the possibility of a recession could mean that more homeowners are going to default on their current mortgages. I also think that it will be challenging for any PMI provider to grow its policy portfolio significantly until rates get closer to or even below 6%.

I rate NMIH as a hold, but it is one that I will revisit when home-buying activity starts to crawl out of its rut and there is a clearer picture of how the current overall economic malaise in the US may come to an end.

data.title: Ellington Credit: A New Entrant To The CLO Sector
data.description:
It doesn't happen often, but occasionally investment funds decide to up and change their mandate. One such case is the Ellington Credit Company (NYSE:EARN) which has switched from a REIT to a RIC with a focus on CLOs. CLOs have been in demand by investors due to their high yields, strong returns and a wide spectrum of risk/reward options. JAAA—the largest CLO fund—has continued to attract interest and has now grown to over $21bn in assets, while a number of CLO Equity CEFs trade at premiums. In this article, we take a closer look at EARN, its new strategy and why it may deserve a look.

On 1-Apr, EARN ceased to be a REIT and converted to a RIC or a registered investment company—the most common corporate structure for investment funds. Unlike a C-Corp, a RIC is not subject to double taxation. Most investment funds are RICs, with the exception of some MLP funds. An interesting detail is that immediately prior to the RIC conversion, the company will operate as a C-Corp. However, it will not be subject to taxes as it has sufficient net operating losses to offset taxable income.

The company expects to maintain its monthly dividend of $0.08. As of the $4.76 price at the time of this writing, that works out to a distribution rate of 20.2%. This will put it roughly in the middle of the CLO CEF pack.

As of Q4, the portfolio was roughly a quarter in CLOs and the rest in Agency MBS. It remains unclear what the fund's actual portfolio will look like at the end of Q1.

Specifically, we don't know the proportion of CLO Equity vs. CLO Debt, we don't know the rating profile of the CLO Debt portion, and we don't know the proportion of US vs EU CLO assets. As of Q4, CLO Equity made up around 2/3 of the CLO portfolio.

There are two key differences between EARN and most of the CLO CEFs. One, it appears that it will maintain a significant amount of CLO Debt in its portfolio. Currently, only EIC and OCCI have a significant amount of CLO Debt, while the rest of the CLO CEFs (5 out of 7) are allocated almost entirely to CLO Equity.

And two, it is the only CLO CEF that allocates significantly across both US and EU CLOs. In our view, this unusual level of flexibility is an advantage relative to the rest of the CLO CEF sector, as it allows for greater opportunity for relative value positioning.

The management fee of the fund is 1.5% of the NAV—defined as "assets minus liabilities". The level of the base fee is the lowest in the CLO CEF sector and is shared by only one other fund. Charging a fee on the NAV is not common for typical CEFs but is fairly common for CLO CEFs. The only difference is that CLO CEFs include preferreds in the NAV figure, which they refer to as the “Equity Base”. At the moment, EARN maintains its leverage via repo; however, this will likely transition to senior securities such as preferreds and bonds at some stage.

Unless the fund uses perpetual preferreds (fairly uncommon for a CLO CEF—there are two instances of CLO CEF perpetual preferreds with the rest being term preferreds) its management fee will be well below the rest of the sector. In effect, it will not charge a fee on its leveraged assets—typically a bit more than a third of total assets—relative to the rest of the CLO CEFs. This will generate a sizable yield advantage and should result in a higher valuation, all else equal. Its incentive fee is 17.5% with an 8% hurdle rate, which is better than all except one other fund in the sector.

Gauging the fund's valuation is tricky for a number of reasons. To know the valuation, we need to have some sense of the NAV. As of Q4, the NAV was $6.53. As of this writing, the price is $4.76 which works out to a discount of about 27%. However, it is nearly certain that the NAV has fallen over Q1 as well, and, if that's correct, the discount is quite a bit tighter than that.

The company sold all remaining non-Agency RMBS and IO-only securities during the fourth quarter. This was a good move as credit spreads moved wider in Q1, which would likely have delivered losses on that portion of the portfolio.

As far as the rest of the allocation, we don't know how the prices of various holdings have moved. We also don't know how much the company's sector allocations shifted over Q1. And we don't know what leverage the company has been running over Q1.

Shareholders approved the RIC conversion on Jan 17, and the portfolio was already a quarter in CLOs by the end of Q4. EARN could have moved swiftly into CLOs in January after the RIC conversion approval, or it could have waited until the conversion was completed on Apr 1.

Gauging the impact of rates, 5y Treasuries have fallen by around 0.5% since the start of the year. That should work out to a loss of 0.02% of book value, as interest rate moves are pretty well hedged. Agency spreads are 0.25% wider. If the entire Agency position was carried through today, that should result in around a 4% drop in NAV.

Moves in CLO Debt and CLO Equity, proxied by ETFs and CEF NAVs year-to-date. Putting everything together suggests a static allocation of around a 14% hit to the NAV. However, a further move into CLOs earlier in Q1 could likely have resulted in a drop in the NAV north of 15%, depending on the leverage. This suggests that the discount could be in the high single-digits. That's still pretty good—it would be the widest discount in the CLO CEF space.

Finally, for what it's worth, Ellington has a strong track record in CLO investments. Since 2012, when the company kicked off its CLO strategy, it has generated an annualized unleveraged return in excess of 20%. That is quite a bit higher than that of the public CLO CEFs.

Having tracked the performance of its mortgage REIT Ellington Financial (EFC) which also has an allocation to CLOs, we were impressed by its resilience over the COVID period. The 10-year drop in the book value of the stock is on the smaller side in the mREIT sector.

Takeaways

Attractive valuation, favorable fee structure in the context of CLO CEFs, strong track record and significant flexibility in positioning across both CLO Equity / debt and US / EUR securities suggest that EARN is now worth a look. There is considerable uncertainty as to what its ultimate portfolio will look like; however, there looks to be sufficient margin of safety in the stock to warrant an allocation at this stage.

data.title: Walgreens Boots Alliance: Last Change To Buy
data.description:
Walgreens Boots Alliance (NASDAQ:WBA) owns the well-known pharmacy chain Walgreens in the U.S. and Boots in the U.K. The past few years have been the most turbulent in its 122-year history. What was once called the Dividend Aristocrat has scrapped its dividend after 92 years (since 1933). Shares have fallen sharply and investors are pessimistic.

I was wrong too. Walgreens was a double blow for me when I wrote about it in 2022 and 2024. Walgreens continues to underperform, putting pressure on margins. Walgreens’ board has approved a takeover bid from PA firm Sycamore Partners, taking Walgreens private after nearly a century on the market.

In my article I look at the growth opportunities of Walgreens and analyze the latest quarterly figures, financial health, dividends and my final thoughts. This will probably be my last article on Walgreens and I’m sad about that.

A Changing Market

The market in which WBA operates has changed considerably in recent years, and unfortunately not in the favour of traditional pharmacy chains. On the one hand, they are struggling with decreasing margins on medicines and increasing labour costs. And consumer behaviour has also changed, they prefer to buy their self-care products online or at discounters. Major competitors such as Amazon and Walmart have set their sights on the pharmacy market with cheap delivery services and competitive prices. As a result, fewer people go to physical drugstores, which puts pressure on Walgreens' revenue.

CVS Health has acquired insurer Aetna and Rite Aid has recently declared bankruptcy. This only makes Walgreens more vulnerable. We see a similar story in the United Kingdom where Boots is active. Competition with online pharmacies is fierce. Online pharmacies such as Shop Apotheke from Germany are expanding into other European countries. The challenging economic climate has hampered Walgreens' growth.

Walgreens has not had much luck in recent years. Walgreens wanted to expand through the merger with Alliance Boots, but the timing was bad. In the years that followed, the trend of online shopping continued to grow.

To stimulate growth, WBA invested billions in VillageMD (GP and diagnostics) near stores. The idea was that this would bring additional traffic to the stores. Ultimately, sales grew, but profits lagged. As a result, WBA had to significantly write down the value of VillageMD. What should have been a growth catalyst, unfortunately, did not.

Private equity firm Sycamore Partners proposed the acquisition in March 2025 to offer $11.45 per share in cash. Shareholders can receive an additional $3 per share from the sale of components such as the stake in VillageMD. This brings the total takeover offer to $14.45 per share (about a 37% premium over the current share price). This is not much more than the book value per share.

Sycamore wants to cut costs, close unprofitable stores and increase value by selling the remaining components. For example, the remaining stake in Cencora (AmerisourceBergen) is being sold for $1.3 billion, as well as the stakes in VillageMD and Boots.

Mixed Quarterly Figures, But Consumers Keep Coming To WBA

WBA’s fiscal second quarter results showed a 4.1% increase in revenue compared to a year earlier. In recent quarters, WBA has consistently beaten analysts’ revenue expectations, which shows that customers still like to go to the pharmacy. However, the higher revenue has not yet translated into profits. But that was largely due to significant write-downs of $4.2 billion, partly on VillageMD. Excluding those write-downs, adjusted earnings were modest at $0.63 per share in the fiscal second quarter. That’s nearly half of the earnings in fiscal second quarter of 2024. Earnings were higher last year due to a tax windfall. In addition, sales of non-prescription items such as cosmetics were weaker. These items often have higher profit margins. Consumers are clearly being more frugal or buying their products elsewhere, perhaps due to increased inflation.

Walgreens is also making progress on its goal of closing 1,200 less profitable stores in three years. The company is doing this to increase efficiency and cut costs. And through sale-and-leaseback transactions, assets are being sold to free up cash.

And its equity has been hit hard by the disappointing results, falling to $7.2 billion from $12 billion a year earlier. At the same time, we see debt and long-term lease obligations that have risen to almost $30 billion, of which $8 billion is in loans and bonds. About $1.4 billion is short-term and must be refinanced within a year. New loans and refinancings are being financed at a much higher interest rate because of the increased interest rates. According to the Federal Reserve’s outlook, policymakers expect a gradual decline in interest rates. By the end of 2025, the Fed Funds rate is expected to be around 3.25-3.50%. That could change if Trump’s tariffs lead markets into a recession.

Final Thoughts

WBA is a name that has been familiar to many Americans for years. For investors it is a symbol of stability and the company offered a rising dividend every year. After 122 years, the curtain seems to fall for WBA on the stock exchange. The stock exchange listing disappears and with it the certainty for those who hoped for recovery.

Nevertheless, this moment offers a unique opportunity. The proposed takeover by Sycamore Partners is attractive. For $11.45 per share, with a possible $3 per share extra, shareholders can part with something special. At the current price, there is an upward potential that has not yet been fully exploited.

With limited downside risk and a visible path to value capture via acquisition, WBA is currently attractive to investors looking to get in now. Unfortunately, this will likely be my last article on WBA, a beautiful company that has been known to Americans for generations.

data.title: Use Tulips To Fund Your Retirement: MicroStrategy Preferred Shares Pay 10% Yields
data.description:
I’m a Bitcoin bull, and I recognize that many readers on Seeking Alpha are not. If you think Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is tulips, you can still gain some controlled exposure to the speculation on BTC for the benefit of your retirement. Strategy, formerly MicroStrategy (MSTR), preferred shares come in two batches: Strike (NASDAQ:STRK) and Strife (NASDAQ:STRF). Both offer around 10% effective yields, paid quarterly. As you’ll see in this article, the yields appear quite safe. Here’s the breakdown.

Strategy Perpetual Strife 10% Preferred Stock (STRF)

Risk averse investors can purchase Strife (STRF), the more senior preferred series of the two. Strategy has committed to ensuring that STRF will be the most senior preferred shares the company will ever issue.

STRF offers 10% yield on the $100 liquidation preference value. This means $10 per share per year dividend should be paid to STRF investors. At a market price of $86.25 as of April 8 close, this is a yield of 11.6%.

Strategy Perpetual Strike 8% Preferred Stock (STRK)

Income seekers who want some possible upside from BTC can purchase Strike (STRK). This pays 8% on the $100 liquidation preference, which comes to a $8 per share per year dividend paid to STRK investors. At a market price of $81.13 as of April 8 close, this is a 9.9% yield.

STRK is special in that each share is convertible into 1/10 a share of MSTR common stock. This means that STRK is an everlasting call option combined with a perpetuity. There are therefore two ways for STRK investors to win:

  • BTC can go up, which sends MSTR higher, which raises the value of STRK’s option value. The floor price of STRK can be thought of as 1/10 MSTR, which is about $23 at a share price of $230. But if, for example, MSTR goes to $1000, then the floor price of STRK becomes $100.
  • Interest rates can go down, which raises STRK’s perpetuity value. Given a fixed income stream, lower rates mean lower discounting which means a larger net present value.

Generally, you can think of the everlasting call option as an option with delta close to 1 as long as implied volatility of the underlying asset is about 80 or higher. An everlasting call option also almost 0 gamma. The high IV requirement isn’t a hurdle for MSTR, which is easily the most volatile large cap stock in the US markets.

Where Does The Yield Come From? Is It Safe?

The yield comes from BTC and is backed by a massive overcollateralization thanks to Strategy’s treasury of $40 billion worth of BTC. Strategy takes the proceeds of the preferred stock issuance to purchase BTC. If BTC rises more than 9.9% or 11.6% each year, then the BTC purchased with those proceeds are enough to fund the dividends sustainably.

What if BTC does not rise more than this hurdle rate? Well, there are only $850 million notional value in STRF issued and $765 million notional value in STRK issued. Senior to both series of preferred stock is $8.2 billion in convertible debt, though about half is ITM and should convert assuming MSTR stays at current levels or goes up.

Thus, the $1.615 billion of preferred stock is covered by a net $32 billion in unencumbered capital in the form of Strategy’s enormous BTC treasury ($40 billion BTC minus $8 billion debt). This is an enormous coverage ratio. BTC must fall quite a bit (over 75%) before the preferred investors are unable to get their money back. And remember that right now, both STRK and STRF are trading at a sizable discount to their $100 liquidation preference.

So the risk of both preferred shares is that BTC does fall over 75%. If you are sure BTC is tulips that are destined to go to 0, then this might not meet your risk profile for long term investment. However, if you are willing to take the view that BTC is tulips which will ebb and flow over time but probably not have a catastrophic, terminal failure event, then either preferred share is likely appropriate for your investment consideration.

There is technically a risk that the company delays dividend payments on STRK. If cash flow is essential to your strategy, then this is a point worth noticing. While it is possible for payments to be delayed on STRF too, the dividend rate obligation compounds with each skipped quarter, up to a max rate of 18% per annum, so it is unlikely that these dividends are skipped.

Just for completeness, I will mention that in practice, the dividends will almost certainly not be paid by cash proceeds of selling BTC. Strategy will likely use the sale of MSTR common to fund dividends or proceeds from future convertible bond issuances. Because the common stock is so exposed to BTC, using an ATM offering is effectively like selling BTC (and technically the BTC would be sold at a premium if the stock trades at a premium to its BTC NAV).

For what it is worth, Strategy insiders are buying the preferred shares.

The Bottom Line

I would rate both STRK and STRF a Hold for the average investor. Given the extreme risk off behavior we are seeing at the macro level, I am somewhat compelled to raise it to a Buy rating since I do believe we could see 10% perpetual yields outperforming most other assets. While I remain bullish on BTC, it is also undeniable that the macro picture looks uglier and stable fixed income may be better for weathering the storm.

So, I don’t think these preferred returns will be particularly strong, but they may very well be stronger than most other investments, especially if the current drawdown in the broader markets continue.

data.title: Xi Jinping Should Have Read 'The Art Of The Deal'
data.description:
They should have read The Art Of The Deal... This is precisely what I suggested to Xi Jinping and the Chinese leadership in my post-tariff announcement article last week. They say the pen is mightier than the sword, and this proved true yesterday. Slapping a 125% tariff on Chinese imports sends a very strong message, not only to China but to every country in the world.

The message is quite clear and has been telegraphed over and over again. America wants fairer trade agreements, and being by far the most important consumer economy, it has an advantage in trade negations, especially if we're actually discussing making trade fair.

But aside from trade, let's not forget that no matter who is the President, the U.S. remains the sole military superpower globally, and many allies will continue relying on the U.S. for support.

I have nothing against China. I like China very much, and I like Xi Jinping, in the sense that he seems like a very smart person and he probably loves his country very much and cares about his people. However, facts remain facts.

China is the U.S.'s primary geopolitical, economic, and military adversary on the global stage. This doesn't mean there needs to be a war or anything. However, China has become the preeminent manufacturing giant globally, and that simply threatens the U.S.'s national interests.

China's Stranglehold On Global Manufacturing

Just look at that chart. Over only the last 25 years, China has gone from exporting almost nothing to exporting nearly $2 trillion worth of goods per year. Last year alone, the U.S. imported nearly $440 billion worth of Chinese exports while exporting only about $144 billion to China.

It's Not 1980 Anymore

After World War II, the U.S. became the preeminent global economic superpower. The U.S. needed to support Europe and other allied nations ravaged by the war. Also, the U.S. had a massive military adversary in the U.S.S.R.

Therefore, it was highly advantageous for the U.S. to have strong allies all over the world, both economically and militarily, to counter communism. The downside to this economic/global order is that too much is done at the U.S.'s expense.

Also, I am not in any way implying that the U.S. doesn't need or want strong allies now. However, the U.S. should not be exploited by other nations through trade, or any other means. There is a reason why the U.S. National Debt is approaching $37 trillion, and part of that reason are the enormous trade deficits.

China Lost The Trade War Before It Started

China lost before the "Trade War" even started. Credit spread widening became concerning, especially with the wild volatility to kick off the week. With almost an 8% intraday swing in the Nasdaq on Tuesday, the market was clearly in a very bad place. The signs of market turmoil, especially in the bond market, may be why the President revealed the 90-day pause on the global tariffs when he did.

Nasdaq 100 Futures: 5-Day Chart

The market actually began recovering on Monday after the unprecedented 3-day capitulation selling event. This incredible drop erased $6.6 trillion of U.S. stock market value on Thursday and Friday alone, and given the steep drop Monday pre-market open, the overall 3-day (peak to trough) decline may have been around $9 trillion, which is by far the most considerable 3 day decline in history.

This Is The Art Of The Deal

The most critical thing in negotiations is to utilize your leverage the best way possible to establish a position of strength to negotiate from. Moreover, if your opponent blunders, you seize the moment, capitalizing on the fact. With all due respect to the Chinese leader, his blunder was not negotiating when the opportunity was present, and making an already bad situation even worse.

At the end of the day, there is not a "Chinese" coalition lining up with China. China is great, but at the end of the day, it remains an adversarial nation to the U.S. and many of its allies, especially like the nations in Southeast Asia that actually fear it.

The European Union will stand with the U.S. as allies at the end of the day, and NATO isn't going anywhere without the U.S. The U.S. is predominantly the backbone of NATO and all of its political and military influence. The EU will make a fair deal with the U.S., especially after seeing what happened with China.

Of course, this is my base case scenario, and things could turn out much worse, but I don't think so. Here's how I see events potentially playing out in the next 1-2 weeks.

The Bottom Line

The stock market could remain volatile, with the potential near term SPX trading range around 5,500-5,200. However, many countries will likely be making new trade deals in the coming weeks, which also coincides with earnings season. Therefore, we could see sentiment continuing to improve, enabling the SPX and stocks in general to move above the crucial 5,500 resistance, and then much higher after that.

The market has many favorable factors like tax cuts, deregulation, potential rate cuts and fiscal stimulus, and much more. Therefore, sentiment could continue improving as we exit this swampy period. While I remain bullish on H2, due to the recent "crash," I've reduced my year-end SPX target slightly to 6,500.

Please Mind The Risks

Of course, there risks like a more considerable slowdown that we expect here, and China may do something unpredictable, as can the EU and other nations. Inflation could go lower or higher and we may have a deflationary slowdown or a potential stagflationary scenario for a bit. Therefore, there are plenty of risks and investors should weigh the risks carefully before investing in stocks here. Always do your own research, and all my best!

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Ares Commercial: Smoked Like Cheap Brisket
data.description:
Ladies and gentlemen, the second quarter of 2025 is already off to a bumpy start. With one of the largest economic disruptions emerging at the start of the second quarter, nearly every asset class is reeling as the market attempts to digest the largest round of U.S. tariffs in the past 100 years. Across the market, asset classes are struggling as the S&P 500 approaches one of the fastest bear markets in recent history.

However, the turmoil of the market has extended outside of large capitalization equities. In fact, some analysts even saw this coming as valuations had expanded considerably towards the beginning of the year. For a while, all seem to be nearly perfect as the market was pricing enormous growth for some of the largest companies in the world. While the beginning of the year saw luster for some of the markets golden children, there are a variety of asset classes that started the year in the doldrums.

One such example was mortgage REITs, which continued to be haunted by the specter of pandemic related woes and the disruptions caused by rising interest rates just several years ago. While the slow-moving commercial real estate market continued to digest a fundamental change to the financing structure of nearly every real estate deal, many mortgage rates continued to face trouble, mainly at the asset level.

Ares Commercial Real Estate (NYSE:ACRE) is one of the largest mREITs trading alongside competing funds like the Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT) and Starwood Property Trust (STWD). Near the beginning of the year, I published an article covering ACRE. In the year prior, ACRE shares had already fallen by nearly 50% on the heels of a distribution cut and growing non-performing loans.

Follow Up

I published the prior article on ACRE on January 14th of this year leading up to the company’s Q4 and 2024 earnings release. I smelled that trouble was still brewing. In contrast, many readers and commenters thought the already beaten down commercial mortgage investor was priced attractively following the turmoil, but I believed “The Worst Could Be Ahead”.

My thesis is outlined below:

Earnings for Q4 and the full year 2024 are coming up in about four weeks. On the heels of information in the commercial lending market that shows the key asset classes are still deteriorating, the fund's performance is unlikely to show material improvement over prior quarters from my perspective. Since the fund reported Q3 earnings, nonperformance across the largest asset classes continues to trend higher than the beginning of 2024. With few drivers of relief in place, funds like ACRE are likely to continue to struggle.

…As it stands today, I think it may be unlikely that the market will foster enough of a comeback to maintain the current payout. Given deteriorating market conditions and ACRE’s Q3 distributable earnings of just $0.07 per share, maintaining a $0.25 per share payout will require improvements.

To summarize, in January, I believe that funds like ACRE were likely to face increasing trouble from the continued rise of non-performing loans. Compounding the issue, the funds distributable earnings of just $0.07 per share didn't seem sufficient to fund a distribution of $0.25 per share per quarter. Without an improving market and with near term earnings falling far short of what is required to meet a sustainable distribution, it was my belief that ACRE was headed lower.

Since the publication and ACRE’s release of full-year earnings, shares have declined by 35% on a combination of factors, including the recent market rout. Over the past ten days alone, ACRE has clocked a decline of nearly 20% as compounding factors lead investors to flee from the mREIT. To add to the pile, management announced on February 12th that the quarterly distribution would be reduced by 40% to just $0.15 per share.

On the quarterly call, CEO Bryan Donohoe mentioned the fund’s commitment to finding a more sustainable dividend amidst underperforming loans and reclaiming non-performing properties through a real estate owned, or REO, process. One example of a property taken back by ACRE is a 607,000 office in North Carolina that ACRE absorbed in Q3 2024 at a carrying value of $59,000,000.

In 2025, we remain squarely focused on further addressing our remaining underperforming loans and REOs and have elected to lower our quarterly dividend to $0.15 per share as our earnings during the year are expected to be impacted by our more flexible balance sheet position...

Nearly three months later, my assessment of ACRE’s future appeared correct more quickly than expected. However, the past is already water under the bridge and the question for ACRE’s investors becomes, what is the right price?

What Looks Good?

With a rapid repricing such as the past three months, investors need to take a fresh look at ACRE and ask themselves if changes in equity value now matches the “real” value of the fund. From one perspective, ACRE is now looking better than ever. As the funds management team expressed, the reduction to the dividend creates balance sheet flexibility which will help ACRE absorb future losses and improve their current balance sheet condition.

At the end of the day, funds like ACRE are mostly comprised of a portfolio of commercial loans and their cash flow. This typically includes first and second lien loans on high-quality commercial real estate around the United States, much of which is new construction.

Over the past year, there have been several improvements to the fund. One considerable improvement for some investors has been the price to book value of the shares which continues to sink lower, currently at 0.37.

The current valuation is around half of the valuation relative to book value at the start of the year. Historically, ACRE has been valued around book value during strong periods such as leading up to the pandemic. The asterisk next to this number is that book value for commercial loans is a relatively malleable number. Loans will generally be valued at par until they underperform, not necessarily expressing risk along the way.

Should the market reconsider their valuation of ACRE and the corresponding assets, there is significant upside in ACRE shares. A valuation reversion scenario would require improvements to the underlying lending market. Over the past year, I have highlighted the turmoil in the commercial markets. I will reiterate a piece of my previous thesis that still stands true:

At the end of last year, CRED-iQ reported that the commercial distress rate reached double digits for the first time in the company’s history. The rising distress rates were led by an increase in office delinquencies, climbing to nearly 16%. While office continues to lead the way as a troubled asset class, multifamily has also continued to struggle following a significant increase in distress rates during the later summer months. Currently hovering above 11%, multifamily is likely to overtake retail as the number two spot for distress rates. Delivering more bad news, hotels are currently coming in fourth place with stubborn distress rates hovering around 9%.

While the market continues to struggle with growing delinquencies and distress rates for commercial loans, there are some signs of life. Bisnow recently reported that delinquencies for bank-owned commercial loans declined in Q4 to just under 2%, the first decline since 2022. Turning a corner on delinquencies and distress rates would mark a significant change in the tides of ACRE.

For those who believe that there are signs of life in the commercial market, it could be time to revisit ACRE.

However, I don’t see this scenario as likely in the near term. Let’s explore why.

Not To Burst Your Bubble

Not to be the bearer of bad news, but the fundamental changes in economics over the past several months spell additional trouble for certain pockets of commercial real estate. A core theme of REITer’s Digest is that not all REITs are created equal. While some are superior to others, there are also different demand drivers that affect each part of the industry differently.

In this case, the discussion is going to be centered around the development-heavy participants in the market. This includes some of the largest multifamily REITs and industrial landlords who have been hungry to build additional space over the past five years due to accommodative macroeconomic tailwinds.

The performance of these companies is tied to a variety of complex, interdependent factors. From one perspective, the value of real estate and the cash flow produced by the assets underpins the foundation of each company and the industry. However, when a fund begins to introduce new strategies, including speculative development, there are additional layers of risk that begin to materialize for shareholders. This risk is primarily centered around factors including forward demand for newly constructed assets and short-term changes in costs, which can dramatically impact the economics of assets currently under construction. Right now, both factors are under fire from a potential trade war.

With tariffs growing and uncertainty rising, forward demand is likely to be impacted in the near term as investors become more wary of deploying capital into risk forward assets. This means many companies which are currently building real estate and the companies which have funded the debt component, including ACRE, maybe in trouble down the line. Just as the pandemic caused the market to freeze, the uncertainty in the market could change the demand outlook for commercial assets across asset classes, particularly amongst international investors who comprise a large portion of the United States commercial real estate market.

Assets that are currently under construction are also exposed to short-term shifts in costs, including material and labor. Material costs are directly impacted by the incoming tariffs, and labor costs are impacted by population flow immigration policies. Over the next several years, I expect the cost of materials and the cost of labor to rise significantly because of tariff associated inflation. While this inflation could lead to an increase in asset prices over the long term, it causes substantial deterioration to the economics of assets currently under development and assets earlier in the pipeline. If nothing else, quick shifts in economics tend to cause the commercial real estate development market to freeze.

A deteriorating or frozen market is the last thing ACRE needs at this moment. Highlighted by the fund’s management on the previous earnings call, the company is hoping to use the next 12 months to rebuild their balance sheet and begin making improvements at the asset level. Additionally, ACRE’s ability to deploy new capital into healthy assets in a stable market will be an important factor in the company rebuilding their brand on a forward basis. With a substantial decline over the past three years, investors of the fund are looking for good news.

Currently, I'm seeing little reason to be excited about shares of ACRE. While the company has once again declined considerably since my prior coverage, there is additional trouble brewing that could cause shares of the company to dive lower once again. While there is opportunity afoot from one perspective with a cheap valuation relative to book value and a reduced payout ratio that frees up working capital, there are simply too many emerging risks to warrant interest in a company which is already struggling. Combining the recent drop in price with a hazy outlook, ACRE earns a Hold rating.

data.title: Kaiser Aluminum May Benefit From US Protectionism While Paying A Decent Yield
data.description:
Introduction

While investors have been through several bouts of market volatility since the turn of the century, many of those episodes, such as the GFC and the collapse of Silicon Valley Bank, were rooted in the banking sector and financial system. And they were eventually largely resolved through actions taken by the Federal Reserve.

This most recent episode of stock market volatility that we’re currently experiencing, however, is not the same; it’s rooted in international trade and the flow of physical goods. And its resolution will probably not come from financial machinations but, rather, eventual physical changes happening in the real world. It is highly likely that when all the dust settles, the current internationalist financial trade model that was initiated with the signing of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (‘GATT’) in 1947 will be replaced by one that sees much more domestic production.

If that occurs, American investors might be in for a bit of surprise to see industries long thought to have gone extinct in this part of the world make a comeback. And while Kaiser Aluminum Corporation (NASDAQ:KALU) never went extinct, it certainly did spend some time on the endangered species list, but it might now be in a position to benefit from the reshoring of American industry.

Kaiser Aluminum

Kaiser was founded one year before the GATT was signed, in 1946, and was soon a fully integrated aluminum producer; from bauxite mining in Jamaica to smelting in Louisiana, Kaiser did it all. But the offshoring of American heavy industry coupled with some poor business decisions along the way eventually led to its bankruptcy in 2002, a process from which it would only emerge four years later in 2006.

It eventually moved away from mining and smelting and is now a midstream processor of raw aluminum. It does value add work, outputting extrusions and flat rolled products, selling them to customers in the automotive, aerospace, and consumer goods industries, among others.

However, over the last couple of years, the company’s overall performance has been unspectacular, with Conversion Revenue coming in at $1.5 billion in both 2023 and 2024 while Net Income hovered at just about $47 million in both those respective years. As can be seen in the chart below, EBITDA took a dip during the Pandemic years but has since recovered to the ~$210 million levels common during the final years of the 2010s.

And the stock price has been nothing to write home about either; over the same time period, it's fallen 44% and has badly under-performed the S&P. The company's market cap is down to a meager $800 million while Enterprise Value sits just above the $1.8 billion mark.

Although its dividend is a bright spot; Kaiser has been paying a 77 cent per quarter dividend since January 2022 and given the pullback in the stock price that $0.77 now amounts to a 6% yield. The company has been paying out dividends consistently for the last 17 years and management has indicated that they intend continuing to do so.

So, on the surface it may seem like there’s not much here, an old industrial company from a bygone era with a falling stock price.

A Shifting Landscape

But the goings on in international trade are quickly causing the aluminum landscape to shift, and that may very well benefit Kaiser Aluminum. However, some investors may be concerned about the extreme swings we’ve recently seen in the US aluminum market.

Prior to the Trump Administration’s imposition of a 25% tariff on all aluminum imports on March 12th, US aluminum prices ran up substantially. Normally the price differential between high-grade (P1020) aluminum delivered to the US Midwest relative to that quoted on the UK-based LME exchange is not that significant, but the impending tariff action resulted in a huge run-up as US consumers rushed to secure metal and front-run the tariffs.

Those tariffs have since come to pass and aluminum prices have tumbled back down, partly resulting from general market turbulence but probably also due to higher inventory levels. Therefore, a logical question is: how will all of that impact Kaiser?

Luckily, Kaiser is able to almost fully insulate itself from changes in aluminum prices by passing those costs onto customers almost immediately. In the recent Q4 2025 earnings call, Keith Harvey, Kaiser’s CEO, mentioned that “…the majority of our businesses are set up to pass through metal [prices] right away. So, you shouldn’t see any drag on our business.” He went onto discuss the matter by saying, “…go back and look at our data when metal was up around $2 a pound. And you can see that our spreads and margins maintain themselves through that period on the way up and on the way down.”

Therefore, Kaiser is price agnostic when it comes to aluminum and is well placed to benefit from greater manufacturing and industrial activity in the domestic U.S. market.

Given that a growing number of OEMs are making statements and discussing their intentions to increase US auto production, one can foresee a probable increase in demand for US-produced aluminum, and Kaiser is ready for new purchase orders. It operates 13 manufacturing facilities, 12 of those in the US, and services customers in wide range of industries.

Kaiser’s management has also been willing to make some strategic acquisitions over the years. In 2018, the company bought Imperial Machine & Tool Co. in a deal that brought it multi-material advanced manufacturing methods and additional intellectual property. And in 2020, it purchased its Warrick rolling mill business from Alcoa Corporation (AA). That facility is located on land still owned by Alcoa and sources its raw aluminum from a nearby Alcoa-owned smelter. This locked-in supply would permit the company to continue growing its slice of an expanding pie.

Risk

The current volatility in the international trade landscape brings with it a good amount of risk for any type of manufacturer and it's currently impossible to predict how rising global trade tensions will play out. The economy could very well fall into a deep recession, severely impacting demand for all types of goods, or the US might eventually enter into trade agreements that do not favor the domestic production of aluminum goods. If these scenarios were to play out, Kaiser's stock price could be negatively impacted.

Takeaway

The reshoring trend that began in the last couple of years looks set to accelerate in the wake of recent U.S. government trade actions. And while this may not be sufficient to launch Kaiser Aluminum’s stock to the moon, it will probably provide the company with solid new opportunities to expand operations, grow revenue, and increase net income as well as cash flows. This should help it fortify its balance sheet, underpin the stock price, and allow the company to continue paying a very healthy dividend. Based on those factors I rate the stock as a Buy.

data.title: Rocket Pharma Out Of Orbit, But Ready For Relaunch
data.description:
Rocket Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:RCKT) is a compelling gene therapy story with two differentiating factors. First, Rocket's therapies seemingly offer a life-long effect as they target cardiovascular and hematology, whereas many gene therapies (especially those targeting liver) display limited durability. Second, the target indications for Rocket's therapies are in diseases with limited or no treatment options with severe unmet needs. We are talking diseases so rare that the patient population for Kresladi is under 1000 in the US/EU. We see real long-term value in the company's AAV RP-A501 program targeting Danon Disease, a severe cardiovascular disease resulting from a type of lysosomal storage disorder that currently requires a heart transplant for real treatment, resulting in high rates of morbidity and mortality.

We see the near-term outlook to be driven by three upcoming clinical milestone expectations, most notably in RP-A601 and RP-A501, and the potential approval of Kresladi (if the company can get through its CRL). With the declining share price over the past year, and recent sell off, we see the current share price of $5 per share as an incredible value proposition for both short- and long-term investment opportunities. Rocket has $372M in cash and will be able to fund operations through the 1H of 2026, likely needing a financing later this year following either positive data readout or Kresladi approval as the company prepares for commercialization.

Excitingly, ahead of commercialization, the company announced in a filing that it has appointed Sarbani Chaudhuri as the new Chief Commercial and Medical Affairs Officer. Sarbani's extensive cell and gene therapy experience through her career, most recently as a VP at Johnson & Johnson, is a critical role as the company moves towards an approved product. Rocket is laying the framework for a successful launch, and this appointment really drives home the company's expectations of success.

We are very bullish and assign a buy rating to Rocket with a price target of $12 in 2025, with a more positive outlook long term.

Milestone expectations for the company in the coming months include:

  • RP-A501 - Program update in 1H 2025
  • RP-A601 - Phase I initial readout in 1H 2025
  • BAG3-DCM - IND Submission in 1H 2025
  • Kresladi Potential Approval in 2025

RP-A501 in Danon Disease

What is Danon disease? Danon disease is an X-linked lysosomal and glycogen storage disorder that results in severe cardiomyopathy. The tragedy of this disease is that the vast majority of patients don't live past 20, with male life expectancy to be approximately 20 years old while female life expectancy is approximately 35 due to later onset disease. There is no cure for the disease (fingers crossed RP-A501) and the only real treatment is a heart transplant, of which only about 20% of patients receive.

Clearly, there is a large unmet medical need for a treatment. RP-A501 is a single intravenous infusion of a gene therapy with a human lysosome-associated membrane protein 2 isoform B (LAMP2B) transgene load. Compared to a highly risky (transplant rejection, renal insufficiency, elevated infection, increased cancer risk) and probably the most invasive surgery one can have, a heart transplant, this has the potential to drastically change the treatment paradigm.

In the company's deck, they estimate that there are approximately 30,000 patients between the US and UK which is a small patient population. Although this may be underestimated, as management estimates only a 30% diagnosis rate. While a small patient population may seem daunting to investors and potential returns, let me highlight one key fact.

THERE ARE NO OTHER INVESTIGATIONAL DD TREATMENTS BEYOND RP-A501.

Now that we have established the need, let's get into the data.

RP-A501 is a AAV9 gene therapy with a LAMP2B load that has been evaluated in a Phase I study in seven male patients with pathogenic LAMP2 variant, cardiac involvement, and left ventricular ejection friction (LVEF) < 40%. The primary endpoints of the study surrounded safety, myocardial LAMP2 transduction and protein expression.

Treatment with RP-A501 was associated with cardiac LAMP2 expression in all evaluable patients at 12 months and demonstrated clinical improvements. Some of the clinical improvements include:

  • Reduction of left ventricular mass index (LVMI) of a median of 24%
    • Normal progression of disease is approximately 8% annual LVMI increase
  • Reduction or stabilization of cardiac biomarkers
    • NT-proBNP - 57% median reduction
    • Troponin I - 84% median reduction
  • Preservation of normalized left ventricular ejection friction
  • Large improvements in quality of life with all patients transitioning to Class I in NYHA class, leaving no clinical symptoms or limitations after treatment

One of the high dose cohort patients required steroid intervention, which may have altered the outcome for the LVMI. As a result, the company is progressing with a lower dosing schedule in the Phase II clinical study for an improved safety and efficacy profile.

The safety results from the study are also acceptable given the disease, with most adverse events occurring within the first 5 months and all serious adverse events within the first 4. Only 3 adverse events were reported beyond the first 12 months post infusion. Importantly, all serious adverse events were resolved without sequelae.

Completing Phase II enrollment in 3Q2024, the company is assessing RP-A501 in 12 patients 8 years and older at a dose lower than the high dose which had concerns from the Phase I study. Management has commented that it hopes to receive accelerated approval with endpoint data at 12 months as support, with full approval coming in tandem with longer follow up. With the current standing of the FDA, it is unknown if this kind of process where an additional trial past accelerated approval would be required, but it is best to lean to the safer side and expect the FDA to require the full trial schedule.

With the Phase II trial enrolling tow pediatric patients, a 3-month observational pre-treatment is required to establish a baseline in patients, which leads to a likely top-line data readout of the study in early 2026. We do expect an update in the coming months on the trial.

The Phase II trial being designed for an accelerated approval, and 12 month efficacy and safety data from the Phase I study really point to the high probability of approval. We expect data from the Phase II study to be slightly better than the Phase I with the singular dosing level that should eliminate concerns from the high dose cohort that were seen previously.

All this to say that an extremely high unmet need in a patient population that dies very young, coupled with no treatment options and promising efficacy and safety data, we are likely to see RP-A501 receive accelerated approval.

RP-A601 in PKP2-ACM

Rocket's other key in vivo asset that is in Phase I is RP-A601 a AAVrh74 based gene therapy for the treatment of Plakophilin 2 in arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy. The asset has robust preclinical data that indicates great potential for Phase I and clinical development.

The company performed POC studies in mice models that demonstrated RP-A601 significantly reduced PKLP2 protein expression in cardiac tissue in tamoxifen-inducible mice leading to:

  • 56% reduction in LVEF
  • 95% increase in right ventricular area
  • 15% fibrosis in left ventricle
  • 38% fibrosis in right ventricle

There are concerns with translation to human studies as humans often carry single-allele mutations, these concerns are mitigated by the lack of evidence that PKP2 mutant proteins exert dominant negative functions and most mutations are truncating variants.

With the Phase I study well underway, we expect to see initial data in the coming months with a keen focus on the safety data as we also look at competitor updates from Trenaya and Lexeo who have similar Phase I programs in PKP2.

Kresladi

Almost a year ago, the FDA issued a CRL to Kresladi around stability and sterility that needed to be validated. We believe that this issue is minor and a BLA of Kresladi is likely to go through as Kresladi's profile is favorable, and the manufacturing process was fine, the CRL being unrelated to either processes.

Kresladi is a therapy designed for the treatment of leukocyte adhesion deficiency type I (LAD-I) that leads to patient death by age 2 in severe cases (75% of patients) and moderate cases patient death by 40 (>50% of patients). This disease prevents leukocytes from adhering to endothelial surfaces at the sites of inflammation because of a gene mutation encoding CD18. Ultimately, this leads to poor wound healing and constant infections that reduce the life span of patients. Kresladi is aiming to improve CD18 expression on leukocytes so that proper adhesion can occur.

Kresladi boasts a promising efficacy profile in its Pivotal Phase I/II trial, with 100% of patients (9/9) having CD18 expression on more than 10% polymorphonuclear leukocytes and 90% having expression on more than 40% at last follow up. This has lead to an approximate 70% reduction in incidence of infections in patients. Further, the company boasts a 100% survival rate for 20-42 month follow-ups in patients. This is important because the primary endpoint for the trials have been around survival exceeding 2 years post infusion, which Kresladi has exceeded.

The safety profile is also favorable, with no Kresladi related serious adverse events.

Risk

While we view the downside risk to Kresladi, RP-A501 and RP-A601 to be relatively low given the favorable safety and efficacy data in these rare disease markets with no treatments, we always have to be wary of additional safety signals. Any significant safety signal can delay or terminate the programs that have so far demonstrated outstanding safety profiles. Kresladi could also see a sub-par launch, if approved, which would be detrimental given the already miniscule market size.

RP-A601 is also facing clinical competition with Lexeo developing LX2020 and Tenaya developing TN-401, which are all targeting the same indication with the same cargo (different vehicles) and are all in Phase 1.

Rocket has also not announced the completion of dosing its program in RP-A501, which finished enrolling last year. This causes concern for the timeline of the program update.

The largest risk I see with the company currently sits at the FDA, which has seen a significant reduction in work force. Experts continue to debate on the impact these changes will have on regulatory approvals and trials, but it is all an unknown. We extend our views on timelines for trials and regulatory meetings with the FDA given the current constraints. Accelerated approval may be a little further out than expected.

Valuation

We value RP-A501 as the true driver behind Rocket's future. With Bloomberg estimates listing peak sales at around $2B, a high probably of success (80) on a 2-year launch timeline, and a 15% discount rate, we get an NPV for RP-A501 of around $8.5B. Turning to the combined secondary programs, we see estimates of peak sales at approximately $1B per year, discounted the same way on a 3-year launch timeline and a much lower overall probability of success of 40%, we get an NPV of approximately $2B. In total, we value the pipeline NPV at $10.5B.

Adjusting the discount to NPV due to pre-approval stage down 80%, we get a valuation of $2.1B and a share price of $21. While optimistic on this price point, we recognize the current market environment and the current price of $5. Our near term price target is roughly half that value at $12 per share.

Conclusion

We believe that Rocket Pharmaceuticals is positioned well for a hard rebound going into the second half of 2025. With the recent market action driving the share price to insane discounted valuations, we see a massive opportunity to pick up an undervalued stock. We have numerous updates for the pipeline coming in the next few months, most notably an update on RP-A501 that will relieve the investor pressure on the stock.

Milestone expectations for the company in the coming months to drive shareholder value include:

  • RP-A501 - Program update in 1H 2025
  • RP-A601 - Phase I initial readout in 1H 2025
  • BAG3-DCM - IND Submission in 1H 2025
  • Kresladi Potential Approval in 2025

The company's programs represent a small addressable market that is severely underserved, while its therapeutics appear to be clinically effective with impressive safety profiles. We expect these safety data to continue forward through readouts.

We still find it hard to believe that the stock is down 50%+ just year to date, compared to the XBI at approximately down 20%.

We are very bullish and assign a strong buy rating to Rocket with a price target of $12 in 2025, with a more positive outlook long term.
